title: Cross-Company Mobility — Moving Between FAANGs at Staff+ Level
description: Most Staff+ engineers at FAANG will change companies every 2-4 years. Cross-company mobility is a strategic career lever — it accelerates leveling resets, compensation jumps, and scope expansion. This article covers level mapping across Google/Meta/Amazon/Apple, culture differences at Staff+, how to interview for Staff+ roles at a different FAANG, compensation porting strategies, and networking across companies.

description.value: The conventional wisdom at FAANG is that tenure signals loyalty, but the data tells a different story at the Staff+ level. A LinkedIn analysis of 10,000+ Staff+ engineers across Google, Meta, Amazon, Apple, and Microsoft shows that the median tenure between job hops is 2.8 years. Engineers who stay at one company beyond 4 years without a level change see total compensation grow at roughly 8-12% annually (largely through refresher grants and stock appreciation), while those who move between companies see 30-50% jumps in total compensation.

Compression is the hidden tax on loyalty. At Google, L6 Staff Software Engineers typically receive annual refreshers of $80,000-$120,000 on a 4-year vesting schedule. If you stay 4 years, your initial grant is fully vested and your refreshers are accumulating, but your annual comp is capped by the refresher model. An L6 moving to Meta as an E6 can negotiate a $600,000-$900,000 initial grant that front-loads 40% in year one. The 4-year math almost always favors the mover — by 30-50% — unless you receive an accelerated promotion.

Beyond compensation, scope expansion is the real reason to move. A Staff+ engineer at Google might own a single API surface or a set of libraries. At Amazon, the same level typically owns a complete service with P&L implications. At Meta, Staff+ engineers move fast and ship to billions — scope is measured in user impact, not lines of code. Each move resets the scope ladder, often giving you larger ownership than you could unlock by staying. For SAP engineers making their second FAANG move, this scope expansion is even more pronounced because you bring a data-platform perspective that pure FAANG engineers lack.

value.title: Stay vs Move at Staff+
value.description: Compare the outcomes of staying at your current FAANG versus moving to another FAANG at Staff+ level over a 4-year horizon.

rows.label: Promotion probability
rows.option2: Reset clock, possibly faster at Meta

description.value: Level mapping between FAANG companies is not a science — it is an art informed by signal calibration. The commonly cited mapping — Google L6 ↔ Meta E6 ↔ Amazon L6 ↔ Apple ICT6 — is directionally correct but hides enormous variance in expectations. A Google L6 Staff Software Engineer typically has 10+ years of experience, leads multi-quarter projects across teams, and is expected to produce design docs that influence the organization. A Meta E6 is expected to ship code weekly, unblock themselves through ambiguity, and demonstrate impact on core product metrics.

The biggest mismatches occur with Amazon and Apple. Amazon L6 SDE is closer to a Google L5.5 — you write significant production code, own a service end-to-end, and write a narrative-style doc for promo every year. Amazon L6 engineers who move to Google often land at L5, not L6, because they lack the cross-team strategic alignment experience that Google demands. Conversely, Google L5s moving to Amazon often get L6 offers because Amazon values individual ownership over consensus-building.

Apple's ICT6 is a special case. Apple does not have a formal Staff+ track in the same way. ICT6 at Apple is a senior individual contributor role, but the bar is set by your org. Some ICT6 engineers at Apple own features that affect millions of users; others own subsystems with no visibility. Apple ICT6 moving to Meta often land at E5-E6 depending on whether they can demonstrate metric-driven shipping velocity. The takeaway: never assume a one-to-one mapping. Interview at the level you believe you can perform at, and let the offer calibrate you.

value.title: Leveling Negotiation: The Level Mismatch Challenge
value.scenario: You are a Google L6 Staff Engineer interviewing at Meta. The recruiter says the initial calibration is E5. Your Google L6 peers say E6 should be the target. How do you handle this?

bad.label: Bad Approach: Accept and Downlevel
bad.text: Accepting an E5 offer from E6 means a 2-3 year delay in reaching your equivalent level. Meta E5 to E6 promotion takes 1.5-3 years on average. You lose the compensation bracket and scope of an E6. More importantly, you set a lower ceiling on your future mobility — future recruiters see your last title, not your last company level.

good.label: Good Approach: Calibrate and Advocate
good.text: Ask the recruiter for specific gaps: 'What would an E6 need to demonstrate in the interview that I did not?' Request a follow-up with an E6+ engineer in the target org. Use your Google artifacts — design docs, promo packet, impact summaries — to build a leveling narrative. Offer to do a 'leveling call' with the hiring committee. If they won't budge, ask for a 6-month performance review with automatic re-leveling to E6 baked into your offer letter.

description.value: Culture at FAANG is not abstract — it dictates how you spend your day, how decisions get made, and how you are evaluated. At Google, the Staff+ engineer operates through influence. You write comprehensive design docs, socialize them across multiple teams, attend dozens of review meetings, and build consensus over weeks. Your impact is measured by adoption of your designs and the quality of technical direction you provide. If you thrive on deep thinking and architectural influence, Google's culture rewards you.

Meta is the opposite end of the spectrum. E6+ engineers at Meta are expected to ship code every week. The famous Meta phrase 'move fast and break things' has evolved, but the expectation of velocity remains. Design docs at Meta are one-pagers, not 50-page novels. Decisions are made in days, not weeks. Impact is measured by user-facing metrics: engagement, retention, latency. The Staff+ engineer at Meta is a force multiplier who ships their own code while enabling their team to ship faster. For SAP engineers accustomed to methodical SAP delivery cycles, Meta requires the biggest cultural adjustment.

Amazon's culture at L6+ is defined by ownership and written narratives. Every proposal is a 6-page doc written in narrative form. Meetings start with 20 minutes of silent reading. The 'disagree and commit' principle means you are expected to push back vigorously during the decision process, then commit wholeheartedly once a decision is made. Amazon L6 engineers own their service's P&L and are expected to think about operational excellence (COE), on-call rotations, and business impact simultaneously. Apple values secrecy, craftsmanship, and cross-functional integration. Staff+ engineers at Apple often work across hardware, software, and design — a unique challenge of integration thinking.

items.label: I prefer deep, uninterrupted thinking time over rapid shipping cycles
I am comfortable writing 6-page narrative documents for every proposal
I thrive on shipping code to billions of users every week
I enjoy cross-functional integration across hardware, software, and design
I can influence without authority through design docs and consensus-building
I am comfortable with ownership including on-call, P&L, and operational metrics

sections.title: Interviewing for Staff+ at a Different FAANG

description.value: The Staff+ interview loop varies significantly between FAANG companies, and the single biggest mistake engineers make is preparing for the loop of their current company and showing up to a different one. At Google, the Staff+ loop includes a 'Googleyness' round (leadership and culture fit), a design round focused on systems thinking, and a 'cross-functional influence' round where you demonstrate how you've aligned multiple teams. Meta's Staff+ loop emphasizes execution history — you must walk through specific shipped projects with measurable impact. Amazon's bar raiser round is specifically designed to test your narrative writing and your ability to defend tradeoff decisions.

The behavioral expectations shift with level. At Staff+, you are no longer being evaluated on whether you can write good code or design a good system. You are being evaluated on whether you can raise the bar for the entire organization. Interviewers at this level ask about failed projects more than successful ones. They want to see how you handled ambiguity, how you made unpopular decisions, how you dealt with organizational resistance. The key behavioral frameworks: Google's 'Leadership at Scale' rubric (staffing, setting direction, execution, and influence), Meta's 'Impact at Scale' rubric (individual impact, team impact, organizational impact), and Amazon's Leadership Principles with a focus on 'Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit' and 'Bias for Action'.

When framing your current FAANG experience in interviews at a different FAANG, focus on transferable scope. An SAP engineer who built a data migration pipeline for S/4HANA should frame it as: 'I designed a system that moved 500TB of transactional data with 99.999% accuracy across 12 regions' — this demonstrates scale, reliability, and system design. Do not assume the interviewer understands SAP contexts. Translate SAP concepts into generic systems-engineering language. A 'transport request' is a 'multi-environment deployment pipeline'. A 'BAPI call' is an 'RPC to a legacy system with idempotency guarantees'. Frame your work in terms of distributed systems challenges.

Compensation porting is the most financially consequential aspect of cross-company mobility. The core mechanism: you use your current total compensation (base + bonus + RSUs at current valuation) as a floor, and negotiate a competing offer to establish a ceiling. FAANG compensation teams are calibrated to market data, but they have significant discretion at the Staff+ level. A Google L6 moving to Amazon L6 can expect a total compensation package between $600,000 and $950,000 depending on the competing offers they bring and how they negotiate the RSU grant structure.

The RSU refresher math is where most engineers leave money on the table. Your current employer refreshes RSUs annually at a grant price. When you join a new company, you receive an initial grant that vests over 4 years (typically 33/33/22/12 at Meta, 25/25/25/25 at Google, 5/15/40/40 at Amazon). The key negotiation lever is the front-loading of the grant. Ask for a 'year-one accelerate' where 40-50% of your RSUs vest in year one. This protects you against the common scenario where you leave a new company in year 2-3 (the median FAANG tenure for external hires is 2.3 years) and forfeit unvested RSUs.

Sign-on bonuses are the second major lever. Amazon offers cash sign-on bonuses that are paid in the first year and, uniquely, a second-year sign-on bonus that replaces the RSU vesting gap (Amazon's back-loaded vesting schedule). Google offers sign-on bonuses but expects them to be repaid if you leave within 12 months. Meta offers signing bonuses that convert to RSUs if you stay. The SAP engineer's advantage: you understand data, and compensation is just data. Build a spreadsheet that models total compensation over 4 years under different join scenarios, factoring in RSU growth rate (8-10% assumed), refresher grants, and bonus targets. Model the worst case (stock declines 20%) and ensure you are still ahead of your current compensation.

sections.title: The SAP Engineer's FAANG Mobility Path

description.value: For SAP engineers, the FAANG mobility path typically has three phases. Phase 1: First FAANG. You join a FAANG company, typically at Senior level (L5/E5/ICT5). This is the hardest step because you must demonstrate that your SAP systems thinking translates to distributed systems engineering. Once you are in, you absorb the culture, build your first FAANG network, and deliver 2-3 high-impact projects that establish your portable reputation. Phase 2, 2-4 years later: Second FAANG. You move to a different FAANG at Staff+ level (L6/E6/ICT6). Your first FAANG experience validates your ability to operate at scale. You now have a compensation baseline, a network of references, and the vocabulary to sell your experience.

Phase 3: Principal/Director or Exit. After your second FAANG, you have options. You can stay and climb to Principal (L7/E7) or Director, leveraging the cross-company perspective that comes from having worked at multiple FAANGs. Or you can exit to a high-growth startup as CTO/VP Engineering, bringing FAANG operational rigor. The SAP background becomes a differentiator here — SAP knowledge at the Principal+ level is rare and valuable. When FAANG companies build enterprise products (Google Workspace competing with SAP Concur, Amazon AWS competing with SAP on cloud infrastructure), your SAP expertise becomes strategic.

value.title: The 'Too Many Moves' Concern
value.scenario: You have changed companies 3 times in 6 years. A recruiter says this pattern is a red flag for Staff+ roles that need stability. Your current company (FAANG #2) is offering a retention package. Should you stay or move?

bad.label: Bad Approach: Ignore the Concern
bad.text: Dismissing the concern by saying 'the market values mobility' signals that you haven't internalized the Staff+ expectation of delivery. A new FAANG investing $1M+ in you wants 2-3 years of sustained output. If your resume shows 1.5-year stints, the hiring committee will question whether you can see a multi-quarter project through to impact.

good.label: Good Approach: Reframe and Commit
good.text: Acknowledge the pattern and reframe it: 'My first two moves were leveling moves (Senior to Staff). I am now at Staff+ and looking for a 3-4 year horizon to deliver my next level of impact.' Back this with a specific narrative of the project you want to complete. If the retention package at your current company sets you up for Principal within 2 years, consider staying. The best mobility strategy is knowing when to sit.

details: Staff+ Insight: Your first FAANG job is the hardest to get. After that, you are in the mobility club. The network you build and the portable reputation you create will define your career trajectory. Every 2-4 years, evaluate: am I learning, earning, or leading? If two of three are flat, it's time to move.
